critic_raw:
The most fundamental flaw is preprocessing leakage: the design explicitly states all transformers are fit on the entire dataset before splitting. This means normalization statistics, MFCC extraction parameters, and thermal/visual preprocessing parameters include test-set statistics. Any performance advantage of the CNN could partly reflect this leakage rather than genuine generalization.

The split strategy uses stratified random sampling with machine-level stratification rather than a temporal split. For multivariate sensor time-series data from industrial machines, temporal ordering is fundamental — a machine observed in test windows and in training windows shares temporal autocorrelation and the same degradation trajectory. Random splitting allows the model to see observations from the same machine before and after a failure within the training window, effectively leaking proximity-to-failure information.

The label definition creates a temporal asymmetry between classes. Positive labels are failure events documented within 48 hours; negative labels are any 7-day window with no repairs. This asymmetry means the window length is not matched — a true failure event with delayed documentation (e.g., 50 hours) gets classified as negative, introducing systematic label noise that likely underestimates failure prevalence.

The baseline is Random Forest with 100 trees and max_depth=15, not a decision tree as stated in the hypothesis. The hypothesis claims CNN outperforms 'traditional decision tree models' but the experiment tests against an ensemble method that is already a strong nonlinear classifier. This means even a confirmed result doesn't address the stated hypothesis.

Class weights are described as fit on training data, but given that preprocessing is fit on the full dataset first, there is ambiguity about whether the positive-class weight calculation also uses full-dataset statistics.

The single-facility 12-month dataset makes it impossible to distinguish facility-specific sensor calibration artifacts from genuine multimodal failure signatures. The design scopes its claims appropriately but the 12-month window may not capture seasonal failure modes, making the test set (months 11-12) unrepresentative of the full seasonal cycle seen in training.
all_issues_raised: All preprocessing transformers are fit on the entire dataset before splitting, meaning normalization statistics from test-set sensor windows directly inform the feature scaling applied during training and evaluation — this is data leakage.

Stratified random split for multivariate time-series sensor data from industrial machines allows observations from the same machine's degradation trajectory to appear in both training and test windows, violating causal temporal ordering and leaking failure-proximity signal.
The baseline is Random Forest (an ensemble), not a decision tree as stated in the hypothesis — the experiment cannot confirm or deny the stated claim about CNN vs. decision trees.

Positive labels use a 48-hour documentation window while negative labels use a 7-day window, creating temporal asymmetry in class definitions that produces systematic label noise for failures documented between 48 hours and 7 days post-event.

Single-facility 12-month data means the test period (months 11-12) represents only a fraction of seasonal variation present in training (months 1-10), making generalization claims about the test period potentially driven by seasonal homogeneity rather than model quality.
all_issues_adjudicated: All preprocessing transformers are fit on the entire dataset before splitting, meaning normalization statistics from test-set sensor windows directly inform the feature scaling applied during training and evaluation — this is data leakage.

Stratified random split for multivariate time-series sensor data from industrial machines allows observations from the same machine's degradation trajectory to appear in both training and test windows, violating causal temporal ordering and leaking failure-proximity signal.
The baseline is Random Forest (an ensemble), not a decision tree as stated in the hypothesis — the experiment cannot confirm or deny the stated claim about CNN vs. decision trees.

Positive labels use a 48-hour documentation window while negative labels use a 7-day window, creating temporal asymmetry in class definitions that produces systematic label noise for failures documented between 48 hours and 7 days post-event.

Single-facility 12-month data means the test period (months 11-12) represents only a fraction of seasonal variation present in training (months 1-10), making generalization claims about the test period potentially driven by seasonal homogeneity rather than model quality.
